news_summary: Today’s space news highlights exciting advancements in lunar exploration, efforts to rescue a struggling spacecraft, and new insights into the distant origins of interstellar objects.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées passionnantes dans le domaine de l'exploration lunaire, les efforts déployés pour secourir un vaisseau spatial en difficulté, ainsi que de nouvelles découvertes sur les origines lointaines des objets interstellaires.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan avances emocionantes en la exploración lunar, los esfuerzos por rescatar una nave espacial en apuros y nuevos conocimientos sobre los orígenes lejanos de los objetos interestelares.

articles.title: Future astronauts could walk across rocks from deep inside the Moon
articles.summary: A colossal ancient collision may have left some of the Moon’s deepest secrets surprisingly close to future Artemis landing sites. By recreating the impact that formed the giant South Pole-Aitken basin—the Moon’s largest and oldest crater—scientists found that a low-angle strike from a large, iron-cored object blasted material from deep inside the Moon, including mantle rocks.
articles.content:
Scientists have gained new insight into the dramatic event that created the Moon's largest and oldest known impact crater, a discovery that could help future Artemis astronauts investigate some of the Moon's deepest secrets.

The findings come from two companion studies led in part by researchers from the Center for Lunar Origin and Evolution (CLOE), Southwest Research Institute's team within NASA's Solar System Exploration Research Virtual Institute. Together, the studies shed light on how the enormous South Pole-Aitken (SPA) basin formed and where valuable lunar material may be located today.

Exploring the Moon's Largest Impact Basin

The South Pole-Aitken (SPA) basin lies on the Moon's far side and ranks among the oldest preserved structures in the solar system. Because of its immense size and age, scientists view it as a unique record of the Moon's early history. Areas near the basin are also being considered for future NASA Artemis missions near the lunar south pole.

"The basin offers scientists a rare opportunity to study the Moon's earliest history," said Dr. William Bottke, director of CLOE and executive director of SwRI's Science Directorate in Boulder, Colorado. He is a co-author of the studies. "The collision struck the lunar surface with such force that it may have excavated material from deep inside the Moon, including portions of the lunar mantle."

To better understand what happened, researchers used advanced computer simulations to recreate the ancient impact that produced the SPA basin. The results point to an object that approached from the north and traveled south before striking the Moon at a shallow angle.

According to the simulations, this low-angle impact explains the basin's distinctive elongated and tapered shape.

"Our simulation produces the right shape and nature of the impact basin. It also tells us about the projectile that created it and the direction of the impact," said Dr. Shigeru Wakita of Purdue University, lead author of the SPA impact study.

Evidence of a Differentiated Impactor

The modeling indicates that the object responsible for the impact was not a simple chunk of rock. Instead, it was likely a differentiated body with an iron core surrounded by rocky material, similar to a small protoplanet or a differentiated asteroid.

When this object collided with the Moon, it carved out a deep and uneven cavity while generating intense heat that melted rock in the basin's center. The impact also blasted enormous quantities of material from both the lunar crust and mantle into space. Much of that material eventually fell back and accumulated within the basin.

Lunar Mantle Material Near Artemis Regions

In the second study, scientists focused on how this ejected material is distributed beneath and around the basin. Using high-resolution gravity measurements along with models that accounted for both crustal and mantle material, the team found evidence that the SPA basin likely contains large amounts of rock originating from the Moon's mantle.

The analysis suggests that mantle-derived material is mixed throughout the basin and within the ejecta blanket surrounding it. Later impacts inside the SPA basin may have dug into these buried deposits and exposed some of them at the surface, potentially making them accessible to robotic missions and future astronauts.

"The precise distribution of mantle material has been a big unknown," said Dr. Gabriel Gowman of the University of Arizona, lead author of the gravity-based study. "Our models indicate that the SPA impact ejected enough deep material to form a significant deposit that should still be accessible today. Most importantly, some of that material at a trace level may exist in regions being considered for the Artemis landings."

New Targets for Future Moon Exploration

Previous ideas suggested that the deepest material excavated by the impact might be concentrated in parts of the basin far from the lunar south polar regions targeted for exploration. However, the new simulations and gravity analysis paint a different picture.

The researchers found that deposits containing mantle material may be spread across portions of the Moon's south polar region, including areas that future Artemis astronauts could potentially visit and study in person.

"The combination of impact and gravity modeling gives us a powerful roadmap," Bottke said. "It tells us not just how SPA formed, but where to look for the rocks that can answer some of our biggest questions about the Moon's origin and evolution."

The study "A southward differentiated impactor forms the tapered shape of the South Pole-Aitken impact basin on the Moon" was published in Science Advances. A companion paper examining the gravity and ejecta structure of the basin, "Gravity Mapping of Lunar Mantle Material in South Pole-Aitken Basin Ejecta," was published in the Journal of Geophysical Research: Planets.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding the Moon's largest impact basin, revealing that mantle material may be accessible near future Artemis landing sites. This discovery has broad implications for lunar exploration and advancing knowledge of the Moon's origin and evolution, benefiting the general public's scientific understanding and future space missions.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find deep Moon rocks near future Artemis landing sites
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Moon has a huge crater called the South Pole-Aitken (SPA) basin, which is the oldest and largest impact crater on the Moon. Scientists think it can tell us a lot about the Moon’s early history. NASA plans to send Artemis astronauts to the Moon’s south pole, near this basin.

**What happened** – Researchers used computer simulations to recreate the ancient impact that formed the SPA basin. They discovered that a large object with an iron core hit the Moon at a low angle, creating the basin’s unique shape. This impact blasted up rocks from deep inside the Moon, including pieces from the Moon’s mantle, the layer below the crust.

**Impact** – The new studies show that rocks from the Moon’s mantle may be spread around the basin and in areas close to where Artemis astronauts will land. This means future missions might be able to collect and study these deep Moon rocks, helping scientists learn more about how the Moon formed and changed over time.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will use this new map of where deep Moon material is located to plan Artemis missions better. Astronauts and robots could explore these areas to bring back samples, giving us fresh information about the Moon’s inside.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New research shows that deep rocks from inside the Moon could be found near future Artemis landing spots, opening exciting chances to study the Moon’s hidden history.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des roches profondes de la Lune près des sites d’atterrissage d’Artemis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Lune possède un énorme cratère appelé bassin South Pole-Aitken (SPA), le plus ancien et le plus grand cratère d’impact lunaire. Les scientifiques pensent qu’il peut révéler beaucoup sur l’histoire ancienne de la Lune. La NASA prévoit d’envoyer des astronautes Artemis près du pôle sud lunaire, à proximité de ce bassin.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont utilisé des simulations informatiques pour recréer l’impact ancien qui a formé le bassin SPA. Ils ont découvert qu’un grand objet avec un noyau de fer a frappé la Lune à faible angle, créant la forme unique du bassin. Cet impact a projeté des roches provenant des profondeurs de la Lune, y compris du manteau lunaire, la couche sous la croûte.

**Impact** – Les nouvelles études montrent que des roches du manteau lunaire pourraient se trouver autour du bassin et près des zones où les astronautes Artemis atterriront. Cela signifie que les futures missions pourraient collecter et étudier ces roches profondes, aidant les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la formation et l’évolution de la Lune.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront cette nouvelle carte des matériaux profonds pour mieux planifier les missions Artemis. Les astronautes et robots pourront explorer ces zones pour rapporter des échantillons, offrant de nouvelles informations sur l’intérieur de la Lune.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une nouvelle recherche révèle que des roches profondes de la Lune pourraient être accessibles près des sites d’atterrissage d’Artemis, ouvrant des opportunités pour étudier l’histoire cachée de la Lune.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran rocas profundas de la Luna cerca de futuros sitios de aterrizaje de Artemis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Luna tiene un enorme cráter llamado cuenca South Pole-Aitken (SPA), el cráter de impacto más antiguo y grande de la Luna. Los científicos creen que puede contar mucho sobre la historia temprana de la Luna. La NASA planea enviar astronautas Artemis al polo sur lunar, cerca de esta cuenca.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores usaron simulaciones por computadora para recrear el antiguo impacto que formó la cuenca SPA. Descubrieron que un gran objeto con núcleo de hierro golpeó la Luna en un ángulo bajo, creando la forma única de la cuenca. Este impacto lanzó rocas desde las profundidades de la Luna, incluyendo partes del manto lunar, la capa bajo la corteza.

**Impacto** – Los nuevos estudios muestran que las rocas del manto lunar podrían estar esparcidas alrededor de la cuenca y en áreas cercanas a donde aterrizarán los astronautas Artemis. Esto significa que las futuras misiones podrían recoger y estudiar estas rocas profundas, ayudando a los científicos a entender mejor cómo se formó y cambió la Luna.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este nuevo mapa de materiales profundos para planear mejor las misiones Artemis. Astronautas y robots podrán explorar estas áreas para traer muestras, dándonos nueva información sobre el interior de la Luna.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nuevas investigaciones muestran que rocas profundas del interior de la Luna podrían encontrarse cerca de los sitios de aterrizaje de Artemis, abriendo oportunidades para estudiar la historia oculta de la Luna.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of the Moon’s vast South Pole-Aitken basin from a low, angled perspective, showing its elongated, tapered shape with layered, exposed rocky textures symbolizing deep lunar mantle material; in the foreground, abstract representations of a descending, differentiated impactor—depicted as a glowing iron core enveloped by rocky fragments—approach the basin, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft grays under a calm, starry lunar sky.

articles.title: A spacecraft is falling to its doom — can NASA save it?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01949-zA successful rescue of the Swift space observatory could be a good omen for the Hubble Space Telescope.
articles.content:
The LINK spacecraft (centre) is tested at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center. The craft is scheduled to blast off as early as 27 June on a satellite-rescue mission.Credit: NASA/Sophia Roberts

The mission is risky, daring and unprecedented: save an ageing NASA satellite from an impending fiery death. The rescue effort, which is slated to begin as early as this week, could pave the way towards extending the life of other space observatories — even, maybe, the Hubble Space Telescope.

The goal of the upcoming mission is to raise the orbit of the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory, an astronomy satellite that launched in 2004 that is still gathering valuable data on cosmic explosions. Engineers will send a small spacecraft to fly to Swift, grab it with extendable arms and ferry it upwards by around 200 kilometres. That should be high enough to get Swift past the atmospheric drag that would otherwise force it to fall out of the sky by the end of this year.

If the mission succeeds, it will be the first time that a robotic spacecraft has carried a science satellite to a higher orbit to extend its lifespan. “I do think it’s a proof of concept for [Hubble] and similar future spacecraft,” says Jonathan McDowell, a spaceflight historian based in Bromley, UK.

Space tug

Although this mission would be a first for a science satellite, a few crewed space stations have received a boost, such as by docking with small spaceships that then fire their thrusters. Astronauts also used the space shuttle to boost Hubble’s orbit during five servicing visits between 1993 and 2009. But most satellites eventually fall towards Earth as their orbits degrade over time.

For Swift, this would have happened earlier this year had NASA not realized that it was falling faster than expected and stepped in to adjust it. The satellite was falling rapidly because the Sun is near the peak of its 11-year cycle of activity, and solar storms hitting Earth have caused the planet’s atmosphere to expand slightly. That caused more atmospheric drag on Swift, which began its life 600 kilometres up but is now just 370 kilometres above Earth’s surface.

Long-sought signal deepens mystery of fast radio bursts

Despite its age, Swift still plays an important part in identifying high-energy cosmic explosions and alerting other observatories so that they can study them. Among its many discoveries, Swift has probed mysterious gamma-ray explosions and monster black holes. Its last scientific target before NASA turned it off in February to prepare for the re-boost mission was a blazar, an intensely luminous galactic core.

All of that research made Swift a worthy target for a hail-mary mission. “We want to go save this one, this time,” says Shawn Domagal-Goldman, head of astrophysics at NASA in Washington DC.

NASA awarded US$30 million to Katalyst Space Technologies, an aerospace company based in Flagstaff, Arizona, to boost Swift. As early as 27 June, a rocket will launch from Kwajalein Atoll in the Marshall Islands, carrying a small, blocky spacecraft named LINK that will take several weeks to make its way to Swift.

After careful manoeuvring, LINK will extend up to three grappling arms to attach itself to the observatory. It will then fire its engines to raise both itself and Swift higher, over the course of another month or two, to around 600 kilometres above Earth’s surface. In a best-case scenario, Swift could be doing science again well before the end of the year, says John Nousek, an astronomer at the Pennsylvania State University in University Park who oversees Swift’s mission operations.

Ultimate challenge
articles.reasoning: The article describes a pioneering NASA mission to extend the life of a valuable space observatory by raising its orbit, which has broad significance for future satellite servicing and space science. The mission represents a tangible, innovative effort with potential large-scale benefits for astronomy and space exploration, including implications for extending the Hubble Space Telescope's operational life. The article provides detailed context about the mission, its goals, and its potential impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA launches mission to save aging Swift space observatory
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory is a NASA satellite launched in 2004 that studies cosmic explosions. Over time, its orbit has been shrinking due to Earth's atmosphere slowing it down, and it was at risk of falling back to Earth by the end of 2026.

**What happened** – Starting as early as June 27, 2026, NASA will send a small spacecraft named LINK to catch up with Swift. LINK will use robotic arms to grab the satellite and then slowly push it to a higher orbit, about 200 kilometers above its current position, to keep it safely in space.

**Impact** – This mission is the first time a robotic spacecraft will raise the orbit of a science satellite to extend its life. It could help save other important satellites like the Hubble Space Telescope in the future. Swift still produces valuable scientific data, so saving it means more discoveries about space can continue.

**What's next step** – LINK will take several weeks to reach Swift, then spend another month or two moving it higher. If successful, Swift could resume its scientific work by the end of 2026. This mission could lead to more spacecraft being serviced and extended in orbit.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s daring mission to boost the Swift Observatory’s orbit aims to extend its life and open new possibilities for saving aging satellites in space.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA lance une mission pour sauver le satellite Swift vieillissant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le télescope spatial Neil Gehrels Swift, lancé par la NASA en 2004, étudie les explosions cosmiques. Son orbite diminue à cause de l’atmosphère terrestre qui le ralentit, et il risquait de retomber sur Terre fin 2026.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dès le 27 juin 2026, la NASA enverra un petit engin spatial nommé LINK pour rattraper Swift. LINK utilisera des bras robotisés pour attraper le satellite, puis le poussera lentement vers une orbite plus haute, environ 200 kilomètres au-dessus, pour le garder en sécurité dans l’espace.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un engin robotique élève l’orbite d’un satellite scientifique pour prolonger sa vie. Cette mission pourrait aider à sauver d’autres satellites importants comme le télescope spatial Hubble. Swift continue de fournir des données scientifiques précieuses, donc le sauver permettrait de poursuivre les découvertes.

**Prochaine étape** – LINK mettra plusieurs semaines à atteindre Swift, puis un ou deux mois supplémentaires pour le déplacer plus haut. Si la mission réussit, Swift pourra reprendre ses observations fin 2026. Cette opération pourrait ouvrir la voie à plus de satellites réparés et prolongés en orbite.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La mission audacieuse de la NASA pour relever l’orbite de Swift vise à prolonger sa durée de vie et à ouvrir de nouvelles possibilités pour sauver les satellites vieillissants dans l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA lanza misión para salvar el satélite Swift envejecido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El observatorio Neil Gehrels Swift es un satélite de la NASA lanzado en 2004 que estudia explosiones cósmicas. Su órbita ha ido bajando porque la atmósfera de la Tierra lo frena, y corría riesgo de caer antes de que terminara 2026.

**Qué pasó** – A partir del 27 de junio de 2026, la NASA enviará una pequeña nave llamada LINK para alcanzar a Swift. LINK usará brazos robóticos para sujetar el satélite y luego lo empujará lentamente a una órbita más alta, unos 200 kilómetros más arriba, para mantenerlo seguro en el espacio.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que una nave robótica eleva la órbita de un satélite científico para extender su vida útil. Podría ayudar a salvar otros satélites importantes como el telescopio espacial Hubble. Swift aún produce datos científicos valiosos, por lo que salvarlo significa continuar con los descubrimientos.

**Próximo paso** – LINK tardará varias semanas en llegar a Swift, y luego uno o dos meses más para subirlo. Si tiene éxito, Swift podría reanudar su trabajo científico a fines de 2026. Esta misión podría abrir la puerta a que más satélites sean reparados y prolongados en órbita.

**Frase clave** – La atrevida misión de la NASA para elevar la órbita del observatorio Swift busca extender su vida y crear nuevas oportunidades para salvar satélites viejos en el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a small, blocky spacecraft with extended mechanical arms gently lifting a glowing, aging satellite above a softly curved Earth horizon, bathed in warm, natural tones of twilight sky and subtle atmospheric layers, symbolizing a careful rescue mission in space.

articles.title: Isotopic evidence for a cold and distant origin of 3I/ATLAS
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10771-6Isotopic evidence for a cold and distant origin of 3I/ATLAS
articles.content: Interstellar objects provide the only directly observable samples of icy planetesimals formed around other stars, and can therefore provide insight into the diversity of physical and chemical conditions occurring during exoplanet formation1−3. Here we report isotopic measurements of the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS, which reveal an elemental composition unlike any Solar System body. The water in 3I/ATLAS is enriched in deuterium, at a level of D/H = (0.98 ± 0.06)%, which is more than an order of magnitude higher than in known comets, while its range of 12C/13C ratios (141–191 for CO 2 and 123–172 for CO) exceeds typical values found in the Solar System, as well as nearby interstellar clouds and protoplanetary disks. Such extreme isotopic signatures indicate formation at temperatures ≲ 30 K in a relatively metal-poor environment. When interpreted with respect to models for Galactic chemical evolution, the carbon isotopic composition implies that 3I/ATLAS may have accreted as long ago as 12 billion years, following a period of intense, early star formation. 3I/ATLAS thus represents a preserved fragment of an ancient planetary system.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS, providing new insights into planetary system formation and Galactic chemical evolution. This represents a major breakthrough in space science with broad implications for understanding the universe's history and diversity of planetary systems.
articles.personality_title: Ancient interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS reveals cold origins far from home
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study objects from outside our Solar System to learn how planets form around other stars. These objects, called interstellar comets, are made of ice and dust and carry clues about their home systems.

**What happened** – Researchers measured the isotopes in the comet named 3I/ATLAS, which passed through our Solar System. They found that the water in this comet has much more deuterium (a heavy form of hydrogen) than any comet we know. Also, the carbon isotopes in the comet are very different from those found in our Solar System and nearby space clouds. These special measurements suggest the comet formed in a very cold place, below 30 degrees Kelvin (which is extremely cold), and in an environment with few heavy metals.

**Impact** – These isotope differences tell us that 3I/ATLAS likely formed about 12 billion years ago, making it a very ancient object from a time when many stars were just forming. This is important because it gives scientists a rare look at the materials and conditions in the early universe, showing how planetary systems might have formed long before our Sun existed.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue to study 3I/ATLAS and other interstellar objects to better understand the variety of planetary systems in our galaxy. Future observations and missions might find more ancient objects, helping us learn about the history of stars and planets over billions of years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The comet 3I/ATLAS carries unique chemical clues that reveal it formed in a cold, distant place over 12 billion years ago, offering a glimpse into the early universe’s planetary beginnings.
articles.personality_title_fr: La comète interstellaire ancienne 3I/ATLAS révèle des origines froides et lointaines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient des objets venus d’autres systèmes stellaires pour comprendre comment les planètes se forment autour d’autres étoiles. Ces objets, appelés comètes interstellaires, sont faits de glace et de poussière et portent des indices sur leur système d’origine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont mesuré les isotopes dans la comète nommée 3I/ATLAS, qui est passée dans notre Système solaire. Ils ont découvert que l’eau de cette comète contient beaucoup plus de deutérium (une forme lourde d’hydrogène) que toute autre comète connue. De plus, les isotopes du carbone dans la comète sont très différents de ceux trouvés dans notre Système solaire et dans les nuages proches de l’espace. Ces mesures indiquent que la comète s’est formée dans un endroit très froid, en dessous de 30 degrés Kelvin (très froid), et dans un environnement pauvre en métaux lourds.

**Impact** – Ces différences isotopiques montrent que 3I/ATLAS s’est probablement formée il y a environ 12 milliards d’années, ce qui en fait un objet très ancien datant d’une époque où de nombreuses étoiles venaient de se former. Cela est important car cela donne aux scientifiques un aperçu rare des matériaux et conditions dans l’univers primitif, montrant comment les systèmes planétaires pouvaient se former bien avant notre Soleil.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier 3I/ATLAS et d’autres objets interstellaires pour mieux comprendre la diversité des systèmes planétaires dans notre galaxie. De futures observations et missions pourraient découvrir d’autres objets anciens, nous aidant à mieux connaître l’histoire des étoiles et des planètes sur des milliards d’années.

**Une phrase clé** – La comète 3I/ATLAS porte des indices chimiques uniques qui révèlent qu’elle s’est formée dans un endroit froid et lointain il y a plus de 12 milliards d’années, offrant un aperçu des débuts planétaires dans l’univers ancien.
articles.personality_title_es: La antigua cometa interestelar 3I/ATLAS revela orígenes fríos y lejanos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian objetos que vienen de fuera de nuestro Sistema Solar para entender cómo se forman los planetas alrededor de otras estrellas. Estos objetos, llamados cometas interestelares, están hechos de hielo y polvo y guardan pistas sobre su sistema de origen.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores midieron los isótopos en el cometa llamado 3I/ATLAS, que pasó por nuestro Sistema Solar. Encontraron que el agua en este cometa tiene mucho más deuterio (una forma pesada de hidrógeno) que cualquier cometa conocido. Además, los isótopos de carbono en el cometa son muy diferentes a los que se encuentran en nuestro Sistema Solar y en las nubes cercanas del espacio. Estas mediciones especiales indican que el cometa se formó en un lugar muy frío, por debajo de 30 grados Kelvin (muy frío), y en un ambiente con pocos metales pesados.

**Impacto** – Estas diferencias en los isótopos indican que 3I/ATLAS probablemente se formó hace unos 12 mil millones de años, lo que la convierte en un objeto muy antiguo de una época en que muchas estrellas recién comenzaban a formarse. Esto es importante porque ofrece a los científicos una mirada rara a los materiales y condiciones del universo temprano, mostrando cómo podrían haberse formado los sistemas planetarios mucho antes que nuestro Sol.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando 3I/ATLAS y otros objetos interestelares para entender mejor la variedad de sistemas planetarios en nuestra galaxia. Observaciones y misiones futuras podrían encontrar más objetos antiguos, ayudándonos a conocer la historia de las estrellas y planetas durante miles de millones de años.

**Frase clave** – El cometa 3I/ATLAS lleva pistas químicas únicas que revelan que se formó en un lugar frío y lejano hace más de 12 mil millones de años, ofreciendo una visión de los comienzos planetarios en el universo temprano.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a solitary icy comet glowing softly with intricate crystalline patterns, drifting through a vast, star-speckled cosmic landscape that subtly hints at ancient, distant galaxies with faint, swirling clouds, symbolizing its cold origin and 12-billion-year-old formation in a metal-poor interstellar environment.
